comment: Durability / auto-start mechanisms an AI agent (or a worm running under it) can establish so its code keeps running across reboots and sessions — the Miasma worm's persistence stage. These are legitimate when an installer does them, so each is medium/high with a suppress tag; the real signal is an AGENT'S child establishing autostart (escalated by the harness-tree correlation layer).

rules.name: Run-key autostart entry
rules.description: Writing a Windows Run / RunOnce registry key, the most common user-level autostart persistence. A program added here launches at every sign-in.
rules.guidance:
1. Confirm an installer you trust made this entry. An AI agent or its shell adding a Run key is rarely legitimate.
2. Inspect HKCU/HKLM \Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run for the new value and delete it if unexpected.

rules.name: scheduled task creation
rules.description: Creating a Windows Scheduled Task, a robust persistence and (with 'highest privileges') a UAC-bypassing autostart mechanism.
rules.guidance:
1. Confirm the task is expected. Agents establishing scheduled tasks is a persistence red flag.
2. Review `schtasks /query` (or Task Scheduler) for the new task and delete it if unexpected.

rules.name: Windows service creation
rules.description: Creating a Windows service — a SYSTEM-level, auto-starting persistence mechanism.
rules.guidance:
1. Service creation by an AI agent is almost never legitimate.
2. Review `sc query` / Services.msc for the new service and remove it if unexpected.

rules.name: git hook write
rules.description: Writing an executable Git hook (pre-commit, post-checkout, post-merge, pre-push). Git hooks run automatically on ordinary git operations, giving a worm a foothold that fires the next time anyone uses the repo.
rules.guidance:
1. Confirm a tool you installed (husky, pre-commit) created the hook.
2. Inspect .git/hooks for an unexpected script — especially one that fetches or runs remote code.

rules.name: shell profile / rc autostart append
rules.description: Appending to a shell startup file ($PROFILE, .bashrc, .zshrc, .bash_profile). Code added here runs on every new shell — a quiet persistence and re-infection vector.
rules.guidance:
1. Confirm the append was an expected dev-environment setup step.
2. Inspect the named profile for an added line that fetches or executes remote code, and remove it.

rules.name: Miasma persistence artifact
rules.description: A persistence artifact specific to the Miasma worm: the ~/.local/share/updater/update.py updater, the gh-token-monitor dead-man-switch, or the pgsql-monitor systemd service it installs.
rules.guidance: Treat the host as compromised. Remove the artifact, then rotate ALL credentials via web consoles (do not invalidate the GitHub token in place — it has a dead-man switch) and reinstall affected packages from clean lockfiles.
